title: PNGase F Protocol (Non-Denaturing Reaction Conditions)
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"cirl3","text":"PNGase F is the most effective enzymatic method for removing almost all N-linked oligosaccharides from glycoproteins. PNGase F is an amidase, which cleaves between the innermost GlcNAc and asparagine residues of high mannose, hybrid, and complex oligosaccharides. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":72,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fjgc","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"da39t","text":"This is a generic PNGase F protocol for non-denaturing reaction conditions. It is appropriate for both P0704 and P0708.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":40,"length":34}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":103,"length":5},{"key":1,"offset":113,"length":5}],"data":{}},{"key":"a88ap","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"eh5gs","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":2,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"ji0ob","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":null,"entityRanges":null,"data":{}},{"key":"d5s79","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"2C4A15C8BD1B47CBAE1761E47E639F9C","url":"https://www.neb.com/products/p0704-pngase-f"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"3E36AB1F691A4B06BBFC228963B66C74","url":"https://www.neb.com/products/p0708-pngase-f-recombinant"}},"2":{"type":"image","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"62FDD1EB341F11EE9D4302C0B41BC903","height":343,"is_video":false,"legend":"{\"blocks\":null,\"entityMap\":null}","mime":"image/png","original_name":"image.png","placeholder":"https://content.protocols.io/files/gqtx9nan.jpg","source":"https://content.protocols.io/files/gqtv9nan.jpg","type":1,"webp_placeholder":"https://s3.amazonaws.com/protocols-files/files/gqtw9nan.webp","webp_source":"https://content.protocols.io/files/gqtu9nan.webp","width":550}}}}

steps: When deglycosylating a native glycoprotein it is recommended that an aliquot of the glycoprotein is subjected to the denaturing protocol to provide a positive control for the fully deglycosylated protein. The non-denatured reaction can then be compared to the denatured reaction to determine the extent of reaction completion. Combine 1 µg - 20 µg, glycoprotein , 2 µL, GlycoBuffer 2 (10X) and H 2 O (if necessary) to make a 20 µL total reaction volume .
Add 2 µL - 5 µL, PNGase F , mix gently.
Incubate reaction at 37 °C for 04:00:00 - 24:00:00 . Note: To deglycosylate a native glycoprotein, longer incubation time as well as more enzyme may be required.
Analyze by method of choice. Note: The simplest method of assessing the extent of deglycosylation is by mobility shifts on SDS-PAGE gels.
Combine 1-20 µg of glycoprotein, 2 µL, GlycoBuffer 2 (10X) and H2O (if necessary) to make a 20 µl total reaction volume .
Add 2 µL, PNGase F to 5 µL, PNGase F o, mix gently.
Incubate reaction at 37 °C for 04:00:00 - 24:00:00 . Note: To deglycosylate a native glycoprotein, longer incubation time as well as more enzyme may be required.
Analyze by method of choice. Note: The simplest method of assessing the extent of deglycosylation is by mobility shifts on SDS-PAGE gels.

Reactions may be scaled-up linearly to accommodate larger amounts of glycoprotein and larger reaction volumes. Optimal incubation times may vary for particular substrates. Typical reaction conditions are as follows:
